sections.directions: Directions: Each set of questions in this section is based on a single passage. The questions are to be answered on the basis of what is stated or implied in the passage. For some questions, more than one of the choices could conceivably answer the question. However, you are to choose the best answer; that is, choose the response that most accurately and completely answers the question.

items.stimulusText:
Studying West African symbol systems may clarify some questions about the development of Egyptian hieroglyphics, a writing system that uses characters in the form of pictures. Two West African systems for writing, Nsibidi and Bamum, for example, may provide insights about hieroglyphics since they, too, form complex writing systems using picture signs. Nsibidi and Bamum differ in that the symbols for one, Bamum, were developed for the Bamum language while the symbols for the other, Nsibidi, are used in a multilingual area of southeast Nigeria among speakers of Ekoi, Igbo, and Ibibio. The histories of each, however, may shed some light on the development of Egyptian hieroglyphics.

Nigerian legend relates that the secrets of Nsibidi were revealed long ago to people by a species of large baboon. This legend, reminiscent of the divine baboon associated with Thoth, the patron of scribes in ancient Egypt, is important because divine inspiration for a language and its symbols is believed to protect both from subsequent changes. As for the significance of the baboon itself, since common beliefs about the same animal are unlikely to be pure coincidence, it would seem that either a specifically Egyptian belief found its way to eastern Nigeria or else an ancient and widely spread African belief was passed to Egypt. It is also possible that the Egyptian hieroglyphics themselves are grounded in previously existing African traditions of graphic symbolism.

With respect to Bamum, Sultan Njoya of Foumban, located near the traditional Nsibidi area, elaborated an independent and original writing system for the Bamum language about a century ago. Njoya initially asked his subjects to draw and name different objects. Njoya then used these pictures to create his first system of writing, containing 466 pictographic and ideographic symbols. He eventually reduced his system to 80 characters, each of which represented a syllable. Using his royal influence, Njoya finally carried his ideas to completion by establishing schools that used his system for reading and writing, by developing a system of printing, and by compiling history texts.

The history of Njoya's system raises the possibility that the form and sudden appearance of Egyptian writing may be explained in a similar way—as the invention of a single person. The progress of a single inventor could have produced the complex structure of Egyptian writing in its earliest recorded form. Hypothetically, the hieroglyphics could have represented sounds rather than meanings from the start, never having gone through earlier stages of functioning solely as illustrations of corresponding objects. The meanings of these picture signs might thus never have been restricted to the original objects they portrayed. Just as Njoya is known to have been impressed, but not influenced, by the Arabic script, so an Egyptian inventor may have been stimulated by the slightly earlier invention of a quite different writing system in Mesopotamia.
items.stemText: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main idea of the passage?

options.optionContent: Nsibidi symbols are similar in form and function to Egyptian hieroglyphics and probably influenced the development of Egyptian pictorial writing.

The hypothesis that Nsibidi symbols and Egyptian hieroglyphics are closely related is supported by traditional accounts of the origins of the two systems and by the extreme improbability that hieroglyphics were invented by a single person.
The development of some West African writing systems may shed light on the history of the development of Egyptian hieroglyphics.
Njoya's invention of a pictographic system of writing provides a model of how hieroglyphics may have developed out of a system of West African pictorial symbols such as Nsibidi.

Recent evidence from West Africa suggests that Egyptian hieroglyphics did not originate as phonetic representations but developed out of earlier pictorial representations of objects related to Nsibidi symbols.

items.stemText: Information in the passage most helps to answer which one of the following questions?

options.optionContent: What influence did Arabic script have on the development of Egyptian and Nsibidi phonetic symbols?
Which developed first, the Nsibidi symbols or the Egyptian hieroglyphics?
What are some practical applications to which Njoya's symbol system was put?
Who is the patron of Nigerian scribes?
Do Nsibidi symbols make up a full written form of any spoken language?

items.stemText: In the passage, the author indicates that Njoya did which one of the following?

options.optionContent: studied Egyptian hieroglyphics prior to his development of a new pictorial writing system, but did not base the latter on the former
developed a system of pictorial symbols to represent the sounds of the words that make up the Ekoi language
produced history texts that helped clarify the relationship between Egyptian hieroglyphics and Nsibidi symbols
developed a writing system that was more consistent with local African traditions as a substitute for the Arabic script previously used to symbolize Bamum words
developed his system of writing partly on the basis of input that he received from his subjects

items.stemText: By calling Njoya's writing system "independent" (first sentence of the third paragraph) the author most likely intends to indicate that

options.optionContent: there is no direct correspondence between the symbols of Bamum and the sounds of spoken Bamum
it did not draw material from any previously existing system of writing
it did not influence the development of any other system of graphic representation
its symbols are not limited to the objects customarily found in the traditional Nsibidi area
it served to represent several languages spoken in a particular geographical area

items.stemText: The author's discussion of Njoya's invention of a system of writing serves primarily to

options.optionContent: give evidence that pictorial writing systems similar to hieroglyphics have developed in Africa without being influenced by hieroglyphics
argue by analogy that the Egyptian system of hieroglyphics might have been developed by a single inventor
provide an example of the creativity of certain inhabitants of the region in which Nsibidi symbols are traditionally used
provide an example of a complex pictorial writing system that was gradually adopted by several cultures
suggest that although Nsibidi seems to be related to Egyptian hieroglyphics, it might instead have developed out of Njoya's system of writing

items.stemText: Based on the information in the passage, which one of the following statements is most likely to be true?

options.optionContent: In developing a system of pictorial writing, Njoya was inspired by the hieroglyphic system of Egypt.
Historians have found evidence that the hieroglyphic system of Egypt was partly derived from a preexisting Mesopotamian system.
Historians have identified communication channels between ancient Egypt and West Africa that help explain the similarities between Nsibidi and hieroglyphics.
The 80-character writing system implemented by Njoya contained none of the symbols originally drawn by his subjects.
Scholars have not found examples of any structurally simple early forms of Egyptian hieroglyphic writing.

items.stimulusText:
Passage A

Charles Darwin is a sterling example of someone who understood the essential tension in science between commitment to the well-established theories and explanations that constitute the scientific "status quo" and willingness to explore and accept new and sometimes strange ideas. It is this tension that underlies the understanding of the history of science as a series of paradigm shifts, in which a set of ideas and theories becomes well-established and widely accepted over time, only to be quickly overturned and replaced by a radically new set of ideas and theories. Such a paradigm shift occurs only when the preponderance of the scientific community is willing to abandon the established ideas and theories in favor of new ones. But such a shift is more than just a revision of the general beliefs and practices of the scientific community; it is also a change in the cognitive framework in the minds of individuals.

Three characteristics of Darwin's intellect and personality mark him as a scientist who found the right balance in this essential tension—a balance that helped him accomplish a major paradigm shift. First, although Darwin had respect for the opinions of his scientific peers, he excelled at challenging authority and thinking for himself. Second, Darwin placed a high value on considering evidence that appeared to run counter to his views. As a result, his critics rarely presented him with a challenge that he had not already addressed. And third, Darwin was able to tap the collective resources of the scientific community and enlist other scientists as collaborators. Darwin's collected correspondence numbers over 16,000 extant letters, most of which involve lengthy scientific discussions.

Passage B

Scientists need to exhibit at least three modes of thought: "curious," "creative," and "critical." These requirements come sharply into focus when one deals with anomalous phenomena. For example, astrophysicists Halton Arp and William Tifft have claimed for years that there is evidence indicating that our interpretation of the redshift of distant astronomical objects—the systematic shift of light emanating from these objects toward the red end of the visual spectrum—is incomplete. Their results, if taken at face value, contradict the usual assumption that the observed redshift of these objects (such as distant galaxies and quasars) is almost entirely due to an ongoing expansion of the universe. Arp and Tifft have been curious in examining strange patterns found in their early observations; they have been creative in trying to devise an interpretation of their results; and they have been critical of their own work by seeking new observations and encouraging others to make independent observations.

However, the astrophysics community has, by and large, applied only the critical mode of thought to the same problem. There have been some attempts to reproduce Arp and Tifft's results, with mixed success, but the general attitude has been "It cannot happen, therefore it does not happen" instead of the more open, "If it does happen, it can happen." As a result, Arp and Tifft have come to be regarded somewhat as heretics.
items.stemText: Which one of the following statements can be inferred from each of the passages?

options.optionContent: Science would progress more rapidly if it were more open to new theories than it traditionally has been.
There have been only a few major theoretical shifts in the history of science.
Major theoretical changes within science have most often been triggered by anomalous observations.
The individual traits of scientists have an impact on the nature and success of their work.
Success in science depends on convincing the scientific establishment that one's theories are creative and original.

items.stemText: Which one of the following is a purpose of both passages?

options.optionContent: to illustrate how conservative the scientific community is in the face of new ideas
to warn scientists that advancing radical new theories may put their careers at risk
to point out the necessity of openness to new ideas in the practice of science
to defend noteworthy scientists against their critics
to illustrate what is meant by a paradigm shift

items.stemText: Both passage A and passage B do which one of the following?

options.optionContent: offer a general view about the nature of scientific thinking
focus on the reaction of the scientific community to a particular theory
criticize the attitude of the scientific community
mention the volume of a scientist's correspondence
refer to a specific view of the history of science

items.stemText: Both passage A and passage B express a favorable attitude toward each of the following except:

options.optionContent: the individual scientist or scientists they discuss
independent thinking by scientists
seriously considering the observations of other scientists
commitment to the scientific "status quo"
consideration of evidence apparently counter to one's views

Passage B

Scientists need to exhibit at least three modes of thought: "curious," "creative," and "critical." These requirements come sharply into focus when one deals with anomalous phenomena. For example, astrophysicists Halton Arp and William Tifft have claimed for years that there is evidence indicating that our interpretation of the redshift of distant astronomical objects—the systematic shift of light emanating from these objects toward the red end of the visual spectrum—is incomplete. Their results, if taken at face value, contradict the usual assumption that the observed redshift of these objects (such as distant galaxies and quasars) is almost entirely due to an ongoing expansion of the universe. Arp and Tifft have been curious in examining strange patterns found in their early observations; they have been creative in trying to devise an interpretation of their results; and they have been critical of their own work by seeking new observations and encouraging others to make independent observations.

However, the astrophysics community has, by and large, applied only the critical mode of thought to the same problem. There have been some attempts to reproduce Arp and Tifft's results, with mixed success, but the general attitude has been "It cannot happen, therefore it does not happen" instead of the more open, "If it does happen, it can happen." As a result, Arp and Tifft have come to be regarded somewhat as heretics.
items.stemText: Which one of the following functions in passage B in a way most like the way that the mention of Darwin's correspondence functions in passage A?

options.optionContent: the point regarding the role of anomalous phenomena in relation to the three modes of thought
the mention of Arp and Tifft's long-standing claim regarding the redshift
the reference to the commonly accepted interpretation of the redshift
the statement that Arp and Tifft have encouraged others to make independent observations
the observation that Arp and Tifft have come to be regarded somewhat as heretics

items.stimulusText:
Passage A

Charles Darwin is a sterling example of someone who understood the essential tension in science between commitment to the well-established theories and explanations that constitute the scientific "status quo" and willingness to explore and accept new and sometimes strange ideas. It is this tension that underlies the understanding of the history of science as a series of paradigm shifts, in which a set of ideas and theories becomes well-established and widely accepted over time, only to be quickly overturned and replaced by a radically new set of ideas and theories. Such a paradigm shift occurs only when the preponderance of the scientific community is willing to abandon the established ideas and theories in favor of new ones. But such a shift is more than just a revision of the general beliefs and practices of the scientific community; it is also a change in the cognitive framework in the minds of individuals.

Three characteristics of Darwin's intellect and personality mark him as a scientist who found the right balance in this essential tension—a balance that helped him accomplish a major paradigm shift. First, although Darwin had respect for the opinions of his scientific peers, he excelled at challenging authority and thinking for himself. Second, Darwin placed a high value on considering evidence that appeared to run counter to his views. As a result, his critics rarely presented him with a challenge that he had not already addressed. And third, Darwin was able to tap the collective resources of the scientific community and enlist other scientists as collaborators. Darwin's collected correspondence numbers over 16,000 extant letters, most of which involve lengthy scientific discussions.

Passage B

Scientists need to exhibit at least three modes of thought: "curious," "creative," and "critical." These requirements come sharply into focus when one deals with anomalous phenomena. For example, astrophysicists Halton Arp and William Tifft have claimed for years that there is evidence indicating that our interpretation of the redshift of distant astronomical objects—the systematic shift of light emanating from these objects toward the red end of the visual spectrum—is incomplete. Their results, if taken at face value, contradict the usual assumption that the observed redshift of these objects (such as distant galaxies and quasars) is almost entirely due to an ongoing expansion of the universe. Arp and Tifft have been curious in examining strange patterns found in their early observations; they have been creative in trying to devise an interpretation of their results; and they have been critical of their own work by seeking new observations and encouraging others to make independent observations.

However, the astrophysics community has, by and large, applied only the critical mode of thought to the same problem. There have been some attempts to reproduce Arp and Tifft's results, with mixed success, but the general attitude has been "It cannot happen, therefore it does not happen" instead of the more open, "If it does happen, it can happen." As a result, Arp and Tifft have come to be regarded somewhat as heretics.
items.stemText: Which one of the following, if true, would most weaken the position of the author of passage B regarding the community of astrophysicists?

options.optionContent: Some members of the astrophysics community have been open to accepting Arp and Tifft's research.
Other researchers have proposed equally radical interpretations of the redshift and have received more positive responses than have Arp and Tifft.
Both Arp and Tifft were originally skeptical about their own observations and interpretations of the data.
Much of Arp and Tifft's work in astrophysics has been of a routine nature and has been accepted by the astrophysics community.
Scientists working in other scientific fields are even more resistant to accepting evidence that conflicts with prevailing theories than are scientists in astrophysics.

items.stemText: Which one of the following ideas from passage A most accurately describes the subject of the last paragraph of passage B?

options.optionContent: commitment to the scientific "status quo"
change in the cognitive framework of the scientific community
willingness to explore and accept new ideas
consideration of evidence that appears to run counter to one's own views
tapping the collective resources of the scientific community

description: The Drill Set contains reading comprehension question types. Each question involves comprehending and analyzing lengthy and complex materials, encompassing both single and paired passages.
